title: Implementing the recommendations of the 2018 External Data Quality Panel | Stats NZ
content:
## Implementing the recommendations of the 2018 External Data Quality Panel
19 July 2023, 1:00pm
This page outlines Stats NZ's response to the recommendations of the 2018 Census External Data Quality Panel (EDQP).
After the 2018 Census, we engaged a group of external data experts to review the quality of the 2018 Census dataset and the methods employed to create it. This group, known as the External Data Quality Panel, produced three reports between mid-2019 and mid-2020. You can read the reports on Data quality for 2018 Census .
As part of their final report, the panel made 24 recommendations to Stats NZ. Some of the recommendations related to the 2018 Census, while others related to future censuses.
The 24 recommendations can be read below grouped under four headings to assist with interpretation of the EDQP’s advice, along with Stats NZ's response to each recommendation. Progress was reported quarterly  during the development of the 2023 Census. This progress update to December 2022 is the final progress report.
- Preparing for the wider release of 2018 Census results
- Ensuring the adequacy of information for users about the quality of the 2018 Census data
- Ensuring greater comparability of data collected in the 2013 and 2018 Censuses data
- Securing the quality of the 2023 Census

### Preparing for the wider release of 2018 Census results
Stats NZ should ensure that users of 2018 Census data have readily available to them:
- Metadata on the relative contributions of different data sources for every variable at all levels in their coding classifications for levels of data aggregation down to SA2 (statistical area 2), and for Level 4 ethnic groups.
- The associated quality rating (at least for Metric 1, data sources and coverage) should be provided at all levels of spatial aggregation (SA2 to region) and for all levels in coding classifications that are frequently employed by users (e.g. L4 for language, L3 religion, L4 for ethnicity, etc.).
Stats NZ acknowledges these recommendations.
For the 2018 Census, the metric 1 (data sources and coverage) quality rating and relative data source contributions for each variable are published at the national level. For variables with hierarchical classifications, the quality ratings and sources have not been published at each level of the classification. Nor have they been published for geographies below national level. Our Customer Services Delivery has information on the data sources quality rating for each variable by geography (SA2 to region) to support customised data requests. Our customer facing service areas have not received any requests to release additional metadata.
For the 2023 Census, we have updated our metadata structure to ensure more consistency in data analysis processes and quality reporting. Metadata will be organised by concept, conceptual variable, and instance variable. Quality ratings will be provided for most conceptual variables, excluding some derivations and input variables, at the national level. We plan to provide information on the contribution of different data sources (data quality rating metric 1) for the conceptual variables at all levels of spatial aggregation down to SA2 level. This information will be made available in data quality metadata published to accompany products and services. We do not plan to provide any other quality rating information at SA2 level. Data quality rating information will not be provided by level 4 ethnicity (or detailed levels of other hierarchical classifications) due to lack of demonstrated customer need for this information. We will continue to monitor customer feedback and are prepared to respond if significant demand is identified.
Stats NZ should work with key users such as Te Taura Whiri i te Reo Māori and Te Mātāwai to clearly communicate the problems with te reo Māori data from Census 2018, the limitations around its usage, and options to futureproof te reo data moving forward.
Stats NZ agrees with this recommendation and has connected with key users to inform around the problems and limitations with 2018 te reo Māori data. We are also working with key users to identify information, quality, and output needs as part of developing the approach for the future. This is reflected in the supply of customised data by iwi affiliation and household composition. Stats NZ notes, however, that even if census te reo data quality is very high, it is unlikely to close the te reo data gaps required for Maihi Māori and Maihi Karauna.
We continue to engage with te reo Māori agencies, including Te Taura Whiri i te Reo Māori and Te Mātāwai to discuss a te reo Māori data plan over the next 20 years (for te Whare o te reo Mauriora) to address gaps in te reo Māori data. This includes ongoing engagement around how we collect te reo Māori data in Stats NZ collections.
Stats NZ should report on 2018 Census SA2 unit source indicator and item source indicator by variable and calculate the metric 1 data quality to support users with an interest in small area analyses, because of the number of small areas experiencing very low coverage and consequently lower data quality across variables.
Stats NZ acknowledges the recommendation. This information was not published for the 2018 Census, but customer feedback was monitored, and we were prepared to respond if significant demand was identified.
For the 2023 Census we plan to provide information on the contribution of different data sources (data quality rating metric 1) for most variables (excluding some derivations and input variables) at all levels of spatial aggregation down to SA2 level. The recommendation to report unit and item source indicator by variable at SA2 is also being considered and we will make a decision in mid-2023 following completion of detailed content design for metadata.
Stats NZ should only make data rated as being of poor or very poor quality overall available where project proposals are considered by Stats NZ on a case-by-case basis, similar to the current procedures for accessing Stats NZ microdata such as the Integrated Data Infrastructure (IDI). This includes data relating to families and households unless Stats NZ determine and advertise a higher quality rating for these data.
Stats NZ agrees that customers should be informed more thoroughly about data limitations of poor and very poor data. To balance data access with informed use, we have made the decision to publish poor data (with appropriate supporting metadata) and to provide very poor data only in ways we can communicate more fully with the customer around the data quality limitations (for example, Data Lab, customised data requests). Very poor data will not be published on our website.

### Ensuring the adequacy of information for users about the quality of the 2018 Census data
Stats NZ should further investigate whether its key methodological assumptions apply at smaller geographic scales.
The 2018 Census dataset consists of census forms received through field collection, supplemented by high-quality administrative records for people from whom a response was not received. These admin enumerations were drawn from a New Zealand resident population using recognised methodologies from the US Census Bureau. The resident population was derived from administrative (admin) data by applying statistical methods to eliminate as much admin over coverage as possible while trying to reduce the risk of also excluding admin records that represented real people that should have been counted. When including admin records, census data were given priority and only records that improved the overall quality of the census data were included.
The supplemented census dataset was assessed against population benchmarks derived using well-established dual-system estimation methodology commonly used for official statistics. The assessment against benchmarks was undertaken for sub-populations defined by demographic variables (age, sex, and ethnic group) and geographic area (Territorial Authority Local Board) and comparisons showed consistency between the trends in the census file and population benchmarks. As summarised in Section 3.6.2 of the Initial Report of the 2018 Census External Data Quality Panel different reviews have looked at the population counts for smaller geographies (meshblocks) and the sensitivity of the method applied in 2018. The EDQP recommended that further investigations are conducted to understand whether key methodological assumptions apply at smaller geographies.
Stats NZ acknowledges this recommendation, however, further scoping of this work indicated that this would be computationally infeasible and the extra value for the 2018 Census is likely to be low. We will take this recommendation into consideration when developing the combined model methodology for the 2023 Census. For the 2023 Census, the design and implementation of combined model methodologies will consider the effectiveness for different sub-populations, including geographic areas, throughout. This may include:
- understanding the quality of admin data for different geographic areas
- consideration of different geographic areas as an input to our decision-making process for adding admin enumerations into the census file
- sensitivity analysis of the design under different response scenarios
- assessment of combined census counts against alternative benchmarks at geographic breakdowns finer than those used in 2018
- improving computational efficiency to feasibly carry out assessments at lower geographies.
Further information on the implications of the 2018 Census methodology on small areas may be available from recommendations 12 and 18.
Stats NZ should consider the costs and benefits of creating a multiply-imputed data set for use by experts working with microdata so that researchers using these data can take account of situations where the relatively high level of imputation will tend to distort relationships between variables and cause uncertainty in estimates to be underestimated.
Exploring the benefits and costs of multiple imputation for the 2018 Census provides a more detailed response to this recommendation. This paper explores the use of multiple imputation for a selected set of 2018 Census variables. We use the results in the paper to provide an example of the benefits of multiply imputed data and understand the challenges associated with such an approach.
Given the findings within this paper, we will not produce multiply imputed datasets for the 2018 Census, nor are we prepared to produce multiply imputed datasets alongside the 2023 Census releases. Stats NZ will continue to build expertise and capability to implement multiple imputation methods in the future.
Stats NZ should continue to investigate and report on issues linked with quality of the 2018 Census data, including up-dating their assessments of variables in the light of analyses of data quality at lower levels in coding classifications.
Stats NZ agrees with this recommendation and will continue to update variables based on a range of analysis, which includes quality. More data quality investigations occurred for the 2018 Census than ever before. Further analysis will be driven by issues as they arise, for example, through customer feedback.
Following additional analysis, we have updated and communicated revised quality ratings for the following variables:
- absentees (was very poor, now moderate/very poor)
- families and households: extended family type (was very poor, now poor)
- families and households: family type (was very poor, now moderate)
- families and households: household composition (was very poor, now moderate)
- number of rooms (was poor, now moderate)
- sector of ownership (was moderate, now high)
- status in employment (was moderate, now high)
- study participation (was high, now moderate)
- work and labour force status (was moderate, now high)
- years since arrival in New Zealand (was moderate, now high)
Variables we have now rated:
- sources of family, extended family, and household income (moderate/poor)
- total family, extended family, and household income (moderate/poor).
Stats NZ should consult with key users of census data to ensure the information they require on data quality is readily available on the Stats NZ website. Information relating to data quality in the 2018 Census should remain visible on the website until at least the results of the 2023 Census are published.
Stats NZ agrees with this recommendation.
We have been engaging with key customers around the 2018 Census data quality issues and challenges. Customer updates have been disseminated progressively as we published information. A suite of methodology papers and metadata to support 2018 data has been published.
A 2018 Census data quality webpage was launched in December 2019 in line with the release of the second EDQP report. Further work was progressed in the first quarter of 2020 to ensure the EDQP’s data quality ratings for variables were displayed alongside the Stats NZ ratings. The data quality webpage continues to be a priority for both digital and communications staff to ensure it remains both visible and useful to 2018 Census customers.
Through the review of the 2018 products and services, data users have also been canvassed about their quality requirements for the 2023 Census.
Stats NZ should collate queries from users about data quality issues and the advice Stats NZ provide, and make this information available to all users, to give users comprehensive information about the quality of 2018 Census data.
Stats NZ agrees with this recommendation.
As part of our standard practice, we ensure all customer queries, issues, and responses are well documented. There are typically a wide range of enquiries, from simple through to complex. Our published metadata products and methodology papers are intended to provide most information relating to quality. All 2018 methodology papers and metadata have now been published. Where appropriate, communications are targeted, for example, around data quality issues to interested customers or particular topics. An example is the release of the 2018 iwi affiliation estimated counts – a joint initiative of the Data Iwi Leaders Group (Data ILG), a subgroup of the National Iwi Chairs Forum, and Stats NZ – to address the gaps in iwi affiliation data in the 2018 Census. Information on data quality is published on Data quality for 2018 Census .
Through our ongoing engagement with customers we hear their views of data quality and impacts. Customers continue to access our online data quality training module.

### Ensuring greater comparability of data collected in the 2013 and 2018 Censuses
Stats NZ should undertake further analysis of the impact for comparisons with 2013 Census data of the changes in the wording or response options of questions in the individual and dwelling forms. Examples include: Tenure of Household, Main means of travel to work, Main types of heating and fuel types used to heat dwellings. A comparison between responses to ‘Main means of travel to work’ and the Ministry of Transport Household Travel Survey data should also be undertaken.
Stats NZ has completed some analysis of 2018 Census data for time-series impacts and informs customers of changes when servicing data requests.
We have advised users that while the final quality rating for the data is moderate due to the low response rate, the data quality and the consistency with expectations and time-series is good. This means the 2018 Census data can be compared with 2013 and 2006 data with some caution.
When using this data, users have been advised:
- Overall, this data may have some bias toward homeowners/family trust households and some under-representation of households who do not own their home or have it in a family trust, including those who rent their home. The patterns and trends seen in this data may not always fully represent the real-world situation or real-world changes.
- There is less data in residual categories for 2018 due to the use of other data sources. Some caution needs to be applied when interpreting time series data because other data sources have been used for the 2018 data that were not used previously.
- Use of administrative (admin) data to identify households who rent their home has improved the quality of the data on renting for 2018, making it more complete than previous data. Care should be taken when interpreting changes in the data as these may be due to the changes in the data collection method rather than real-world change. Some households included in the renting category for 2018 may have previously been in the not stated category.
We have undertaken analysis of the impact of changes to the travel to work question and advised users that looking at proportions rather than levels is more appropriate. Comparing proportions show a similar distribution of responses in 2013 and 2018, with some expected changes such as an increase in working from home and use of public transport. Given the change in concepts, we are satisfied that the data are broadly comparable.
In response to customer need, the question wording and response options collected were changed for the 2018 Census to provide a clearer picture of heating use. Main types of heating have not previously been collected, so the 2018 data is not comparable with previous censuses.
Time-series analysis can be done for fuel types used to heat dwellings, but the 2018 fuels data is not fully comparable with previous data. We have advised users that for time series analysis on gas use, the mains gas and bottled gas categories in the previous data can be combined and compared with the gas category in the 2018 data. Users have also been advised that the number of dwellings where no heating is used compared with previous censuses is likely to have increased in 2018, due to text responses such as ‘none’ or ‘no heating’ being coded to ‘no heating used’, which had not happened previously.
We will continue to monitor customer feedback and be prepared to respond if significant demand is identified.
Stats NZ should investigate the possibility of recalculating 2013 Census results using (as far as possible) 2018 Census methods. The retrospective use of government administrative records from around the time of the 2013 Census could contribute responses where non-response has been high, and enable more consistent measurement of changes that have occurred in comparable census data between 2013 and 2018 than is currently possible.
The retrospective use of 2018 Census methods to fill in missing data where non-response was high in the 2013 Census could result in more consistent measurements of change between the 2013 and 2018 Censuses. Measurements of change with improved consistency would better enable us to understand inter-censal change. Stats NZ has investigated the possibility of recalculating the 2013 Census results using as much of the 2018 Census methods as possible.
- Although we were able to recalculate aspects of the 2013 Census results using the 2018 Census method, we did not have confidence in the final results because key elements of the methodology could not be implemented.
- Māori population under-estimation in 2013: Analysis and findings provides us with the best 2013 population estimate to analyse results against. We were able to obtain a national count similar to the alternative Estimated Resident Population (ERP) calculated in the paper ‘Māori population under-estimation in 2013: Analysis and findings’.
- Breakdowns by smaller geographies, sex, age, and ethnicity of our final dataset did not, however, match with our expectations and the findings from ‘Māori population under-estimation in 2013: Analysis and findings’. This was most likely a result of the limitations and changes required to implement the methodology.
There were several limitations in this project, which prevented us from implementing the full 2018 Census method.
- The 2018 Census methodology used historical (2013) census data as an alternative data source. So, the 2006 Census data would be required to implement this methodology for the 2013 Census. We were unable to use 2006 Census data to fill in missing information because it is not linked to the IDI spine. This would limit information for several variables that relied predominantly on previous census data, for example ethnicity, Māori descent, birthplace, and religious affiliation.
- Some admin data sources that were used in the 2018 Census were not included because they were either not available or not a reliable data source at the time of the 2013 Census. This resulted in lower quality of location information from admin data sources. As a result of the lower quality of location information we are less likely to correctly place individuals from admin data in their correct geographical location.
- Unoccupied dwellings account for 10.6 percent of all dwellings in the 2013 Census and are not linked to the IDI spine. Because of this, we do not have information on geographic location for unoccupied dwellings. This negatively impacted our ability to link individuals with strong evidence that they should be part of the usual resident population to unoccupied dwellings.
- Imputation of remaining missing data using the Canadian Census Edit and Imputation System (CANCEIS) was not possible within the DataLab. It was required that all work for this project be done in the DataLab since the data is unit record data. The DataLab infrastructure does not support the ability to run CANCEIS and it would be too resource intensive to emulate CANCEIS’ functionality through other tools.
It is not possible to implement key elements of the 2018 Census methods to recalculate the 2013 Census results. While further work can be done to improve our results, further assumptions and changes to the 2018 Census methods would be required. As a result, our understanding of inter-censal change would remain limited, especially at smaller geographies. Any observed differences may be due to the assumptions and changes made as opposed to real-world differences. Given this, we will not be producing an alternative 2013 Census dataset based on the 2018 Census methods used to fill in missing data. We acknowledge that this is a loss to understanding inter-censal change between the 2013 and 2018 Censuses, as well as a loss in the time series that will be used to analyse and disseminate alongside the 2023 Census.
Stats NZ should systematically investigate the impact of the use of alternative data sources (previous census data, data from a range of admin sources, imputed data) on the quality of data across variables. Analyses should focus not just on whether population distributions are in line with expectations, but also impacts on estimates of inter-censal change, the impact on the sizes of ethnic groups and small areas (e.g. SA2s), and the impact on bivariate associations between variables.
For this recommendation Stats NZ chose to focus on the potential impact of alternative data on bivariate associations as minimal information is available about this topic. See the response to Recommendation 10 in relation to our attempts to understand the impacts on inter-censal change.
Proposed method to investigate changes in bivariate associations as a result of using alternative data in the 2018 Census explores the impact of the use of alternative data on bivariate associations. This report gives researchers that are using 2018 Census microdata an insight into the possible impacts that alternative data may have on the associations between variables of interest to them. It provides researchers with a method to test if the use of alternative data has an impact on bivariate associations.

### Securing the quality of the 2023 Census
Stats NZ should ensure data collection in future censuses is comprehensive enough to accurately measure iwi affiliation, and should take responsibility, in partnership with iwi, for investigating alternative ways to measure iwi affiliation so that the census is not the only source.
The field design for the 2023 Census has a key focus on driving high Māori response rates, by putting investment into solutions that will make it easier for respondents to complete census forms. This includes more Māori field staff (including a greater focus on local staff), so that the field operation can have a greater emphasis on contact with and assistance for respondents, and far more extensive use of paper forms. Our field model also aims to increase data equity through differential approaches based on respondent need. For example, our most intensive intervention stream, Assist, is targeted at those households and communities that had low response rates in 2018, and without change, will likely continue to have low response rates in 2023. These are predominantly our priority response groups of Māori, Pacific Peoples, ethnically diverse communities, and young people. The objective is to produce census data that both represents national and local communities and meets output quality needs.
Stats NZ agrees with working with iwi to investigate alternate ways to measure iwi affiliation. Stats NZ and the Data Iwi Leaders Group of the National Iwi Chairs Forum signed the Mana Ōrite Relationship Agreement in October 2019. Alongside the associated Mana Ōrite Work Programme, work continues across the business to improve administrative data to ensure a sustainable and diversified flow of relevant iwi data for Māori. Stats NZ chairs a cross-agency group – Iwi and Māori Data Need – which meets quarterly. In particular, two of the agencies represented in this group have significant work underway in regard to iwi affiliation. The Department of Internal Affairs is developing a paper – He Tātai Tupuna – that investigates whether the government should collect iwi affiliation of Māori as part of the national and official records system. The Ministry of Health is developing Tātai, an online platform where Māori can share iwi affiliations / information that will eventually be shared back to iwi to progress towards self-determination. Stats NZ works closely with these agencies and others represented in this working group to ensure we provide support with any mandated standards where relevant.
In the broader context of the current Mana Ōrite work programme progress includes iwi-led collections operational design for 2023 Census (Te Mana Whakatipu) led by Te Kāhui Raraunga, in partnership with Stats NZ. This partnership will test innovative, decentralised approaches to census collection. As a result of this initiative, we anticipate better whole-of-community and iwi engagement and response rates in three locations – the Ōhua iwi collective in the Far North, the Toitū Tairāwhiti iwi collective, and by Te Whānau ā Apanui. It is anticipated that the census data collected will both build capability for iwi and improve response rates for Māori. An evaluation report of what is learned will be shared and will benefit both Iwi Māori and Stats NZ when collecting and measuring iwi affiliation in future censuses.
If admin data is not available or limited in 2023, then the accuracy of iwi affiliation data from the census will depend on the response rates we can achieve with available funding. The 2023 Census: High Level Design includes a key focus on driving high Māori response rates.
Stats NZ should prioritise engagement and investment to ensure:
- There is genuine partnership with Māori communities, organisations and iwi to develop and implement decision-making and governance mechanisms, to ensure meaningful involvement of Māori in future censuses. This includes Stats NZ actively addressing the acceptability of the extensive use of administrative data in future censuses and issues of social license and Māori data sovereignty specifically for the 2023 Census.
- There is a real voice for members of all communities, especially Pacific peoples and new migrants, in decision-making on data about them, including the use of admin data in the census.
An enduring relationship with the Data Iwi Leaders Group (Data ILG) of the National Iwi Chairs Forum has been established through the Mana Ōrite Relationship Agreement signed with Stats NZ in October 2019.
Governance board representation, coupled with the work being progressed under the Mana Ōrite Relationship Agreement, forms part of our focus to address concerns raised by the External Data Quality Panel regarding Māori data sovereignty. We now have our Kaihautū – Te Tohu Rautaki Angitu Māori and a Data ILG representative on the 2023 Census Governance Board.
Te Taumata Whakaritenga rōpū has been established and meets monthly. The primary objective of Te Taumata Whakaritenga is to enable greater transparency and Census programme accountability for Māori and iwi, and advice, assurance, and influence on decision-making on significant deliverables across the programme. The Census programme is predominately at the “Inform” end of Te Arawhiti Engagement Framework and continuing to develop capability towards collaboration and co-design to enable genuine influence of design and planning of census initiatives. Positive sentiment has been received from Te Taumata Whakaritenga.
Monthly hui with the Data ILG discussing census items under the Mana Ōrite work programme continues to progress. This has included the implementation of a geospatial map in the Internet Collection System (for online census forms) for the iwi affiliation question in addition to auto-coding rules and solutions to known issues from 2018.
Hāpaitia te Whakaaro, the 2023 Census engagement with Māori plan, guides Census programme engagement with Māori through tikanga and application of te ao Māori so the relationships built are meaningful and enduring. Raising awareness of census, the combined census model, the value of census, and participation in census are enhanced through relationship principles articulated in the plan.
The 2023 Census Treaty statement sets out the Census programme’s commitments as a Treaty partner.
Stats NZ is continuing to determine the best option with the available funding to maximise outcomes for Māori from the 2023 Census. This focuses on delivering quality data for and about iwi and Māori and building iwi-Māori capability to collect their own data. Budget 2021 confirmed an additional $14.1m for Te Mana Whakatipu (iwi-led collections and data analysis). The key outcome of this work is to build iwi-Māori capability and capacity to collect and analyse data.
The community engagement and communications workstream for the next census will focus on understanding, maintaining, and building trust and confidence, including in relation to the use of admin data. The use of admin data in the production of statistics (including the census) will be incorporated into engagement with iwi, Māori, and other communities (informed by regional and tactical engagement plans for eight regions covering the entire country). A 50 percent increase in engagement resources, to 48 advisors recruited from the communities they will be supporting, will provide greater reach. Engagement supports community expectations that their feedback on the use of admin data informs decisions about the 2023 and 2028 Censuses.
Stats NZ has developed a Pacific Engagement Approach. Two Pacific specialist roles have been established to implement the approach over the next three years. Memorandums of Understanding have been signed with the Pacific Data Sovereignty Network and the Ministry of Pacific Peoples to support a Pacific footprint within the data system and data collection.
Stats NZ should ensure individual census responses from prisoners are obtained in the 2023 Census.
Stats NZ agrees with this recommendation.
The 2023 Census model includes a specific focus on improving the response rate and quality of data collected from non-private dwellings, including prisons and prisoners.
We have worked with Department of Corrections at the national level on a plan and requirements for enumeration of all prisoners which is being implemented for the collection operation.
Stats NZ should ensure that all collection instruments (paper and online forms), systems and processes are thoroughly reviewed, tested, and made fit-for-purpose for 2023, including an assessment of the equity implications of all collection instruments (paper and online forms), systems and processes. It is essential that issues with the 2018 collection instruments are addressed for the 2023 Census. In this context, Stats NZ should review the extent to which the way the online forms were administered contributed to missing responses in 2018, with a focus on the differential impacts for different population groups and consider whether changes are needed for the 2023 Census.
Stats NZ agrees with this recommendation.
The review, design, and testing of systems, services, and processes to ensure they are fit-for-purpose for the 2023 Census is essential.
The field model for the 2023 Census includes significantly greater availability of paper forms than 2018, alongside an approach to assist those that would benefit from help completing their census forms.
As noted in the Report of the Independent Review of New Zealand’s 2018 Census, the Internet Collection system for the online forms was secure, stable and easy to use. Stats NZ has made improvements to the online system to ensure ease of accessibility and that it is fit-for-purpose. An accessibility audit of the online forms against the upcoming Web Content Accessibility guidelines version 2.2 (WCAG 2.2) has been undertaken. The paper form has also been assessed against the Accessibility Charter, and cognitive testing has been undertaken. Where possible, changes identified from these assessments have been incorporated into the forms.
Stats NZ should report on data quality at the small area (SA2 (statistical area 2)) level to support analysts and policy makers with an interest in small area analyses and build quality rating calculations by Level 1 ethnicity for every variable relating to individuals into their quality assurance and evaluation plans for the 2023 Census.
For the 2023 Census we plan to provide information on the contribution of different data sources (data quality rating metric 1) for most variables (excluding some derivations and input variables) at all levels of spatial aggregation down to SA2 level. The Warrant of Fitness assessments that support quality assurance and evaluation will include metric 1 quality ratings for level 1 ethnicity for variables about individuals and where relevant for dwellings and families and households.
Stats NZ should ensure that the methodology to be adopted for the 2023 Census makes explicit provision for high quality measures of intercensal change between 2018 and 2023. The following actions are recommended to avoid further breaks in time-series for census data:
- A high-quality Address Register should form the basis of a management information system for the field and online enumeration to support a quality management strategy that would allow early intervention when things go wrong or not as planned.
- Because the method of field collection of responses will remain a critical part of the next census the enumeration model for 2023 must be developed by building on that which worked in 2013 and earlier rather than that which failed in 2018 and led to unprecedented quality problems.
- Continue to impute for missing item non-response.
- Assess any new changes to methodology very carefully against whether they would lead to further disruption to the census time-series.
- Review the content of the census forms to ensure the information needed to assess the quality and integrity of data is present. An example is reinstating the question on the total count of people in a household/dwelling.
- With appropriate public consultation and attention to privacy and data justice impacts, make use of access to the relevant government administrative records in advance of the next census to maximise response rates by, amongst other things, targeting field operations, the distribution of paper forms, the number and location of field staff etc.
- Should reliance on administrative records grow, reassess how far statistical surveys(Household Labour Force Survey, New Zealand General Social Survey, and Household Economic Survey), may be better placed to obtain some of the information traditionally gathered by the five-yearly census, such as household and family statistics.
- Provide where possible for measures of quality of the enumeration to be an integral part of the census collection and estimation stages as they proceed.
- Consider the role of the PES in 2023, given the changes in census methodology introduced for the 2018 Census.
- Consider producing household and families data by ethnicity.
- Work has been undertaken to improve the quality of the Statistical Location Register (SLR), which is the source of the Operational Frame. The treatments to improve the SLR and Operational Frame, including desktop canvassing, commenced in October 2021 and continued through to the first extraction of the frame in October 2022. There has been ongoing quality improvement work since October 2022 which will finish on 30 January 2023, when the final extract occurs and the data is loaded into Salesforce for the 2023 Census live operation. The Operational Frame will feed into the management information system that will be used to monitor the census operation.
- The 2023 field collection model builds on what worked well in the 2018 Census and includes changes to address all of the major issues identified (provision of more paper, more field staff, a revised recruitment approach, local ownership of work and strengthened community engagement and communications). The model also uses what worked well in 2013 and earlier, with a greater emphasis on contact with and assistance for respondents in areas with historic non-response primarily.
- Our design of the 2023 Census includes the use of statistical imputation, where appropriate, for items when no other valid information (from a census response or alternative source) is available. Editing, data sourcing, and imputation: Planned approach for the 2023 Census is now available on the Stats NZ website.
- Stats NZ agrees and the census design team has considered the implications of new changes.
- The review of content for the 2023 Census is complete and the 2023 Census: Final content report is now available on the Stats NZ website. The question on number of census night occupants will be reinstated on census forms (rather than derived, as in 2018).
- The 2023 Census: High Level Design includes a scope statement that there will be limited use of admin data for field operational efficiencies (other than for development of a fit-for-purpose address list). Admin data will not be used for any further field operational efficiencies (such as occupancy status). Data from the 2018 Census and the Deprivation Index will be used to drive stream assignment. Projected number of dwellings based on the address list will be used to inform distribution of paper forms.
- Stats NZ is currently exploring the future architecture of population and social statistics through a programme that will join together a number of streams of work, including the contribution of statistical surveys and admin data, to deliver high-quality social statistics in a sustainable way.
- The operational intelligence tool is now live and is being used by the 2023 Census Programme. It will be used to monitor how the collection operation is progressing and trigger actions to respond where things are not tracking as planned.
- In 2023, census is planning to incorporate admin enumerations and admin data by design to mitigate missing data where it can. The Post-enumeration Survey (PES) will therefore base measurement of person coverage on this combined census model with a particular focus on doing this by design. There are a number of key elements of coverage assessment that differ when considering a combined census compared to a traditional census model. These elements were highlighted in the 2018 PES report as areas for research and investigation, the outcomes of which have formed the basis of a PES ‘Standard Design’. This has been endorsed by a Quality Assurance Panel (comprising internal and external coverage experts). The standard design is scheduled for publication in March 2023. There has also been a focus on research and investigation on key areas where risk mitigation controls might be required, or to inform alternative options if assumptions do not hold. This will form the basis of our ‘adaptive design’ which is scheduled for publication in June 2023.
- A conceptual methodology for defining families and households data by ethnicity has been developed. The methodology will be able to be applied and included in data content with products and services.
Stats NZ should support a dedicated team for the 2023 Census to undertake postprocessing for families and households data, and other complex variables, and not divert this team to other tasks.
Some of the Manual Intervention processing team will be dedicated to family and household coding until this is completed. We are also looking to bring forward family coding in the process so that issues are identified before further processing is completed.
Stats NZ should continue to undertake WoF assessments for variables in the 2023 Census and should implement a systematic template for WoF content which should include quality ratings for regional councils and Level 1 ethnicity. Quality control processes should be implemented to ensure assessments are of a consistently high standard.
Stats NZ agrees with the recommendation and will continue to undertake the Warrant of Fitness (WoF) assessments. The WoF template for 2023 has been reviewed and content for new standard templates have been developed for units, attributes, and concepts in the families and households topic. The templates include metric 1 quality ratings for regional councils and Level 1 ethnicity for all concepts about individuals and where relevant for dwellings and families and households.
Quality control processes are in the process of being finalised and will be in place to support the analysis phase. These include quality gates, guidelines on quality assessment, and the WoF review and problem resolution processes.
Stats NZ should have an organisational commitment to, and focus on, achieving effective partnership with Māori to develop a census delivery model that will achieve a very high response (>94 percent) from Māori in the 2023 Census.
Stats NZ agrees with the intent of the recommendation.
The goal of the 2023 Census is to deliver high quality data, in particular driving high Māori response rates through engaging strongly with Māori communities and putting investment into solutions that will make it easier for respondents to complete census forms.
Our second Investment Objective is to provide customers with timely and equitable access to rich and statistically sound data to support nationally and locally significant decisions. The two relevant measures of this objective are:
- Accuracy – collection of at least 90 percent of responses from forms (individual and dwelling) at the national level.
- Māori response – evidence of confidence amongst Māori partners and other key Māori interest parties that significant improvements in Māori responses has materialised.
Stats NZ will work with Treaty partners to ensure we maximise the opportunities within our funding.
Budget 2021 confirmed an additional $14.1m for Te Mana Whakatipu (iwi-led collections and data analysis). Led by Te Kāhui Raraunga, in partnership with Stats NZ, this will allow Stats NZ and iwi to test innovative, decentralised approaches to census collection. We anticipate better whole-of-community and iwi engagement and response rates in two rohe (locations) as a result of this initiative.
The 2023 Census Treaty statement sets out the Census programme’s commitments as a Treaty partner.
Stats NZ should set response rate targets for particular Territorial Authority and Auckland Local Board areas and ethnic groups that had low response rates in 2018. These targets will drive a focus and resources into areas/groups needed to achieve a much more balanced and complete response profile in 2023 than was achieved in 2018.
The design of the 2023 field operation began with a focus on areas and ethnic groups that had particularly low response in 2018. This aims to ensure the areas and populations with historic low response and the greatest barriers to participation in the census will get the most intensive collections treatment (collector effort and assistance) to boost participation. We have included variables to account for low 2018 participation rates and Māori and Pacific populations in this design and we are now being guided by the programme’s Priority Response Groups. We also plan to use increase interviewing assistance (over 2018 use) to support completion of census forms. Planning is currently based on 15 percent of the population getting the most intensive treatment and a further 15 percent getting an increased degree of assistance (over 2018) including contact at the doorstep. Community engagement activity will also be most intensive in these areas to support awareness, full participation, and assistance.
We have targets in place to track progress and will monitor and analyse ethnic groups, Auckland Local Board Areas and other Territorial Authorities progress (response and operational progress) during the field collection.
Stats NZ should review the priority ratings that it gives to variables for the 2023 Census so that key statutory duties, including in respect of Māori (e.g. te reo Māori), and variables used to construct units of analyses (such as households/families), are reflected in higher priority for the associated variables.
Priority ratings are reviewed in readiness for each census. Priority ratings for concepts in the 2023 Census have now been reviewed and can be viewed in the 2023 Census: Final content report (refer to the appendix).
Stats NZ should ensure that external scrutiny, in advance of the 2023 Census, is focussed on methodology, field planning and systems for interaction with the public as well as quality management and quality measures. Ex post reviews, such as that of the External Data Quality Panel, can contribute to improvements in future censuses, but for the 2023 Census it would be more cost effective if some of that external expertise were applied well in advance of the enumeration.
Stats NZ agrees with the essence of this recommendation. Upon reflection we have decided not to form an ongoing External Data Quality Panel, but to bring in external experts to review, challenge, and advise on key aspects of the 2023 Census programme as required.
